coredata.dc:title: Waste-to-resource: Utilization of carbon dots derived from proliferating Sargassum aquifolium (Turner) C. Agardh for fluorescent detection of levofloxacin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yuxue
Wu, Luyong
Niu, Yanyan
Li, Huihui
Sun, Tingting
Ye, Miantai
Wang, Lisi
Wang, Baoli
Shi, Fan
Yao, Yucen
Sun, Wei
coredata.dc:description: The recurrent proliferation and blooms of Sargassum have emerged as a major environmental challenge in tropical and temperate coastal regions, with profound implications for ecological balance, economic stability, and societal well-being. Levofloxacin (LVF), a fluoroquinolone antibiotic, is physiologically toxic and its residues and accumulation in the aquatic environment have become a serious threat to ecosystems and human health. This work presents a novel approach for the fluorescent detection of LVF, a common antibiotic in environmental waters, using carbon dots derived from the abundant invasive seaweed Sargassum aquifolium (Turner) C. Agardh. The Sargassum carbon dots (SACD) were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method and can reduce the internal filtering effect (IFE) of LVF, thereby facilitating fluorescence detection of high-concentrations of LVF. SEM and TEM were used to characterize the structure, and SACD exhibited a spherical amorphous carbon with a partial lattice structure. The composition of SACD was further analyzed by using FTIR and XPS after synthesis. This method offers a wide detection range (1 μM–100 μM, R2 = 0.998) and low detection limit (0.3 μM) for LVF analysis, exhibiting high recovery rates (103.4 %–113.9 %) in spiked real environmental water samples, demonstrating its potential application performance. The SACD can selectively detect LVF in the presence of the common interfering substances. Therefore, SACD has promising applications in environmental pollutant detection. This study proposes a sustainable approach to utilizing and mitigating the impact of invasive seaweeds, highlighting the potential for converting biological waste into valuable resources for environmental monitoring and protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Levofloxacin
Fluorescent probes
Carbon dots
Sargassum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image of SACD. Inset: the partial lattice fringes of SACD. (B) Full X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum and high-resolution C 1s (C), O 1s (D), and N 1s (E) spectra of SACD.

Contour plots for the excitation-emission matrices of SACD in HAc-NaAc buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.0) at varying concentrations: (A) 0.005, (B) 0.01, (C) 0.03, (D) 0.05, (E) 0.10, (F) 0.25, and (G) 0.50 mg/mL, demonstrating FL peak position shifts, along with (H) their corresponding UV-Vis absorption spectra.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of SACD suspensions (0.05–0.50 mg/mL). Inset: Excitation (λem = 448 nm) /emission (λex = 361 nm) spectra (0.25 mg/mL SACD). (B) Comparison of emission spectra of LVF (concentration range: 0.001–0.2 mM) in the absence and presence of SACD (λex = 361 nm). Insets: I) Contrast of maximum fluorescence intensity (Fmax); II) Visualization under UV 365 nm light: (a) SACD only, (b–e) SACD combined with LVF (0.001–0.2 mM), (f–i) LVF (0.001–0.2 mM) control samples. (C) SACD + LVF mixture emission spectra with LVF (0–100 μM). Inset: Fluorescence-concentration correlation. (D) CIE diagram of SACD-LVF (0–100 μM) mixtures under 361 nm excitation.
(A) The ultraviolet (UV)absorption of SACD andLVF. (B) The fluorescence lifetimesboth in theabsence and presence of LVF. (C) Mechanism of sensing for SACD and LVF.
Fluorescence intensity of SACD mixtures with interfering substances, under 361 nm excitation in the presence and absence of 0.1 mM LVF.
Preparation procedure of the SACD and the fluorescence detection of LVF.
Comparison between SACD and reported detection methods for LVF.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The determination and recovery rates of LVF in lake water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Waste-to-resource: Utilization of carbon dots derived from proliferating Sargassum aquifolium (Turner) C. Agardh for fluorescent detection of levofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Haikou Key Laboratory of Marine Contaminants Monitoring Innovation and Application, Haikou Marine Geological Survey Center, China Geological Survey, Haikou, Hainan 571127, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The recurrent proliferation and blooms of Sargassum have emerged as a major environmental challenge in tropical and temperate coastal regions, with profound implications for ecological balance, economic stability, and societal well-being. Levofloxacin (LVF), a fluoroquinolone antibiotic, is physiologically toxic and its residues and accumulation in the aquatic environment have become a serious threat to ecosystems and human health. This work presents a novel approach for the fluorescent detection of LVF, a common antibiotic in environmental waters, using carbon dots derived from the abundant invasive seaweed Sargassum aquifolium (Turner) C. Agardh. The Sargassum carbon dots (SACD) were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal method and can reduce the internal filtering effect (IFE) of LVF, thereby facilitating fluorescence detection of high-concentrations of LVF. SEM and TEM were used to characterize the structure, and SACD exhibited a spherical amorphous carbon with a partial lattice structure. The composition of SACD was further analyzed by using FTIR and XPS after synthesis. This method offers a wide detection range (1 μM–100 μM, R2 = 0.998) and low detection limit (0.3 μM) for LVF analysis, exhibiting high recovery rates (103.4 %–113.9 %) in spiked real environmental water samples, demonstrating its potential application performance. The SACD can selectively detect LVF in the presence of the common interfering substances. Therefore, SACD has promising applications in environmental pollutant detection. This study proposes a sustainable approach to utilizing and mitigating the impact of invasive seaweeds, highlighting the potential for converting biological waste into valuable resources for environmental monitoring and protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) represent a novel class of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, characterized by their spherical morphology and tunable nano-size (size range spanning from several to tens of nanometers) [1,2]. Derived from abundant and non-toxic biomass resources, biomass CDs predominantly consist of fundamental elements including carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen [3]. Their remarkable optical characteristics, coupled with high conductivity, minimal toxicity, superior biocompatibility, resistance against photobleaching, exceptional water solubility, and eco-friendliness, have attracted substantial interest.

Transforming marine-derived bio-waste into CD-based functional materials not only promotes resource recovery and utilization [4], but also aligns with the ecological imperatives outlined in “the United Nations Decade of Ocean Science for Sustainable Development (2021–2030): Implementation Plan” and “the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development Goals” [5,6]. Conventional methods employed for biowaste valorization encompass thermal conversion, anaerobic digestion, and composting among others. As the urgency for sustainable and environmentally benign management of biowaste intensifies, exploring greener avenues for its utilization becomes increasingly paramount.

The marine brown alga Sargassum aquifolium (Turner) C. Agardh (Fucales, Sargassaceae) is found to contain various phytoconstituents, including carbohydrates, tannins, steroids, and saponins, among which tannins and steroids are identified as the most predominant constituents [7,8]. Sargassum, a prevalent seaweed, proliferates in oceanic environments, and is frequently washed ashore and disposed as garbage [9], leading to recurrent environmental pollution [10–12]. Consequently, it is imperative to investigate methods for harnessing the waste resources of Sargassum. Prior research has explored and implemented strategies for utilizing Sargassum [13]. It suggests that CDs derived from Sargassum exhibit superior properties [14]. For example, these CDs display exceptional optical characteristics, function as water-based lubricants [15], and demonstrate antimicrobial effects when incorporated into Sargassum carbon nanodot–ZnO nanocomposites [16]. However, there is a scarcity of reports on the application of Sargassum-derived CDs in antibiotic sensing research.

The widespread utilization of antibiotics has given rise to heightened concerns regarding environmental water and food safety [17], posing substantial risks to human health through the emergence of drug resistance [18] and allergic reactions [19]. Among these antibiotics, LVF stands out as a particularly problematic fluoroquinolone due to its high stability and incomplete metabolic breakdown, leading to persistent residues in environmental waters [20,21]. This accumulation presents a significant global threat to ecosystems, highlighting the critical need for effective detection and analysis of LVF in environmental samples [22].

Various techniques have been developed for pharmaceutical detection, such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [23], electrochemical sensors [24–26], and chemiluminescence [27]. These techniques are commonly employed for detecting LVF residues, but often suffer from complex operational procedures and high equipment costs. In contrast, fluorescent methods [28–30] offer several advantages, including straightforward material synthesis, ease of operation, low cost, and high sensitivity. These benefits make fluorescent methods an attractive alternative for detecting and analyzing LVF in environmental water.

Scheme 1 of this report illustrates the application of a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method for the direct and efficient synthesis of low-toxicity, environmentally friendly Sargassum aquifolium (Turner) C. Agardh CDs (SACD). These CDs of this study are innovatively employed in the fluorescent detection of the antibiotic LVF. The underlying mechanism was elucidated through experimentation. This approach offers a broad detection range, high sensitivity, robust anti-interference capability, and good selectivity. In actual sample tests, it demonstrated impressive recovery rates, underscoring its significant practical value in detection applications.

Unless otherwise noted, all reagents employed in this study were of analytical grade and purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd, which were utilized without any further purification. Ultrapure water obtained from Milli-Q IQ7000 water purification system (Merck, USA) was used in all experiments. Sargassum aquifolium (Turner) C. Agardh was collected from Fisherman's Wharf in Qinglan Bay, Wenchang City, Hainan Province [31].

The morphologies of the as-prepared materials were analyzed using a JSM-7100F scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired from a Tecnai G2 F20 X transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured with an ESCALAB 250 Xi X-ray electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on an RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and three-dimensional fluorescence spectra were acquired using a FluoroMax Plus fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba, Japan). UV absorption spectra were obtained from a U-3900 UV–vis spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) with infrared absorption spectra obtained using Nicolet 6700 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA).

SACD were prepared with minor modifications to the procedure described in reference [32]. Initially, Sargassum leaves were dried at 60 °C and then finely ground. Subsequently, 3.77 g of the ground material were boiled in 375 mL of water, yielding approximately 120 mL of residue. This residue underwent sonication for 30 min and was then subjected to hydrothermal treatment in a conventional Teflon-lined steel autoclave at 200 °C for 12 h until it naturally cooled. The hydrothermal products were centrifuged multiple times at 10,000 rpm for 10 min each to separate the precipitate from the supernatant. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane before dialysis.

The sample was placed into a 3000 Da dialysis bag and immersed in a beaker containing 1L of ultrapure water, where it was dialyzed under magnetic stirring for 24 h. During this period, the water was replaced every 4 h. Finally, the contents in the dialysis bag were collected, designated as SACD and stored at 4 °C for future use. The concentration of as-prepared SACD was determined to be 1.5 mg/mL through freeze-drying.

Solutions of LVF at various concentrations (100 μL) were mixed with 2900 μL of SACD suspension (0.25 mg/mL) in HAc-NaAc buffer (100 mM, pH 4.0). After incubating for one minute at room temperature, the fluorescence emission spectra of these mixtures were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 361 nm.

Lake water samples were collected from the Guilinyang campus of Hainan Normal University and pretreated following the methods described in previous literature [33]. In brief, the supernatant of the lake water samples was obtained after centrifugation and filtered through a 0.22 μm filtration membrane. Subsequently, different concentrations of LVF were spiked into the samples and diluted with a HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4.0) for subsequent use.

Sargassum aquifolium (Turner) C. Agardh leaves were employed as raw materials for the facile preparation of SACD via a hydrothermal method. TEM (Fig. 1 A) reveals that SACD, with an average particle size of approximately 5 nm (Fig. S1), consists of spherical amorphous carbon exhibiting a partial lattice structure with an interplanar distance of 0.11 nm [34,35]. The SEM images of SACD, both in the absence and presence of LVF have been shown Fig. S2. It can be seen that the agglomeration degree of SACD increases after the addition of LVF.

The surface composition and elemental analysis of the resultant SACD were characterized using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. 1B) exhibited three peaks at approximately 285.12, 400.18, and 532.07 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The SACD was found to consist of elements C1s, O1s, N1s, and Ca2p, with relative percentage compositions of 50.71 %, 20.36 %, 3.32 %, and 1.52 %, respectively. The presence of Ca may be derived from bio-based calcium of Sargassum [36].

The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1C) can be deconvoluted into three component peaks with binding energies at approximately 284.8, 286.13, and 288.45 eV, attributed to C–C/C=C, C–O/C–N, and C=O, respectively. Additionally, the O1s spectrum (Fig. 1D) showed two peaks at 531.63 and 532.54 eV, assigned to C=O and C–OH/C–O–C, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) displayed two peaks at approximately 400.01 and 401.38 eV, corresponding to C–N–C and N–H bands, respectively. Consequently, the XPS data are consistent with those reported in reference [37], indicating that the nanodots prepared from Sargassum are SACD-containing hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino functional groups.

FTIR spectra of SACD, LVF(aq), and their mixture, which illustrate the respective functional groups, are presented in Fig. S3. The broad peak at about 3415 cm−1 is attributed to O–H/N–H bonds. The peaks in the range from 1475 cm−1 to 1270 cm−1 can be assigned to C–H bonds. [38–41]. The peak at about 1635 cm−1 corresponds to C=O bonds, characteristic of carboxyl or amide I groups, consistent with the carbohydrate and protein components of Sargassum used as carbon sources for synthesis [40,42,43]. The strong peak at about 1110 cm−1 is assigned to C–O bonds. A peak at 621 cm−1 has been assigned to aromatic compounds with sp2 hybridizations [44].

As-prepared SACD (1.5 mg/mL) displayed excitation-wavelength-dependent (Fig. 2 and Fig. S4) and concentration-dependent (Fig. 2 and Fig. 3 A) photoluminescence analogous to certain reported CD nanomaterials [45,46], likely attributable to defect state emission (surface traps) and intrinsic emissions (electron-hole recombination, quantum effects sites) [47,48]. Concentration-dependent analysis (Fig. 2) reveals a dual emission behavior: the 450 nm emission peak undergoes gradual intensification under constant 360 nm excitation, while the 500 nm peak displays synchronous enhancement accompanied by progressive excitation wavelength redshift, as evidenced by the corresponding UV absorption redshift. Initially dominant at lower concentrations, the 450 nm emission undergoes intensity inversion at elevated concentrations, where the 500 nm peak becomes predominant.

The functional groups present in SACD act as excitation energy traps, leading to diverse fluorescent properties by introducing various defects on the surface throughout the hydrothermal carbonization process. This is likely the origin of the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior [49].

Under 361 nm excitation used in the experiments, due to the internal filtering effect (IFE) of LVF [50], in the absence of SACD, the fluorescence intensity initially increased significantly with increasing concentrations of added LVF, followed by a subsequent decrease (Fig. 3B, Inset I). As a result, concentration ambiguity in direct LVF autofluorescence detection, one fluorescence intensity may match two different concentrations, posing a risk of misjudgment. In contrast, in the presence of SACD, the fluorescence emission intensity of high-concentration LVF was significantly enhanced. Maybe this enhancement is due to the reduction of the IFE of high-concentration LVF by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between SACD and LVF, as reported in the literature [51]. Obviously, SACD has the ability to identify high-concentration LVF, avoiding misjudgments caused by IFE. Therefore, in this study, SACD was adopted to detect LVF to expand the sensing range.

Consequently, SACD can function as a fluorescent probe to detect LVF. The experimental conditions, including material dosage, the type of buffer solvent and pH value, were subsequently optimized to enhance detection performance. Optimization studies identified a 0.25 mg/mL SACD suspension (Fig. 3A) as the optimal concentration, exhibiting maximum fluorescence emission intensity at 448 nm under 361 nm excitation used for subsequent analyses. As illustrated in Figs. S5–S6, in contrast to the optimal pH of 6.0 for as-prepared SACD, the peak response of SACD to 0.1 mmol/L LVF is achieved in an acetic acid–sodium acetate (HAc-NaAc) buffer environment at a pH level of 4.0. Therefore, the subsequent experiments utilized a 0.1 M HAc-NaAc buffer with a pH value of 4.0. The detection capability of SACD for LVF was then examined under these optimized conditions. It was observed that as the concentration of LVF increased in SACD, there was a significant enhancement in the fluorescence emission intensity (Fig. 3C).

Moreover, upon examining the CIE chromaticity diagram, it becomes evident that the fluorescence color of SACD undergoes a transition from blue to green with the increment in the concentration of LVF (Fig. 3D).

Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 3C, the max fluorescence intensity of SACD + LVF mixture demonstrates a robust Boltzmann nonlinear regression correlation in relation to the concentration of LVF within the range from 1 to 100 μM. The specific fitting equation is presented as follows: y = 670.21–961.55/(1 + exp((x-14.74)/29.09)), accompanied by an R2 value of 0.9984. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) for LVF with the utilization of SACD is ascertained to be 0.3 μM, which is determined based on a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3 [52].

Table 1 illustrates different analytical methods of LVF. This indicates the utilization of SACD is facile, and expeditious, which demonstrates a relatively low detection limit, high sensitivity and wide detection range compared to other investigations reported in the literatures.

As previously noted, as the concentration of LVF in SACD increases, the fluorescence emission peak gradually red-shifts from 448 nm to 503 nm (Fig. 3B). The peak at 503 nm is essentially consistent with the emission peak wavelength of LVF, suggesting that this peak can be attributed to LVF.

As presented in Fig. 4 A, the UV absorption band of LVF overlaps with that of SACD, which promotes energy transfer between the two. Moreover, fluorescence lifetime experiments were conducted to explore the fluorescence properties of SACD in the absence (2.09 ns) and presence (5.77 ns) of LVF, as demonstrated in Fig. 4B. The UV absorption and the significant alteration in fluorescence lifetime imply the occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between LVF and SACD [51].

The FTIR functional group analysis results suggest that the SACD surface is abundant in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups (Fig. S3), which enhance water solubility and hydrogen bond interaction with LVF (Fig. 4C). FTIR spectral shifts show that upon mixing LVF with SACD, both the O–H/N–H (compared to pure LVF) and C=O (compared to individual LVF and SACD) stretching vibration peaks exhibit redshift (shift to lower wavenumbers) and broadening, indicating hydrogen bonding between SACD and LVF [58,59].

Significantly, the fluorescence emission peak of SACD at 503 nm was gradually enhanced with the augmentation of LVF concentration. This phenomenon could potentially be ascribed to the hydrogen bonding interaction and FRET between SACD and LVF, as proposed in the literature [49]. Consequently, SACD was chosen as the LVF fluorescent probe on account of its interaction with LVF.

The selectivity of the SACD and LVF mixture system to various common interfering substances, including cations, anions, antibiotics, hormones, and pesticide molecules (Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, K+, Mn2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, SO4 2−, Br−, CN−, SCN−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, chloramphenicol, melatonin, glyphosate) was examined. As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity of the LVF-containing and LVF-free SACD, respectively, showed only slight differences in the presence or absence of these interfering substances. These findings suggest that SACD can selectively detect LVF in the presence of these interfering substances. Moreover, it also validates the potential application of SACD in the detection of environmental pollutants.

SACD was employed to determine LVF content in lake water samples, thereby confirming its potential in monitoring the concentration of LVF within environmental pollutants. As presented in Table 2 , the relative standard deviations (RSDs) of the detection results are lower than 2 %, and the spiked recoveries of LVF from the lake water samples span from 103.4 % to 113.9 %. These results imply that SACD represents a dependable approach for detecting LVF in lake water samples. Consequently, SACD exhibits substantial potential for detecting LVF in environmental pollutants.

In this study, invasive Sargassum was exploited to fabricate CDs with various particle sizes, which exhibited extensive excitation and luminescence features. These properties endow them with the capacity for the fluorescent detection of the antibiotic LVF in water, thereby addressing environmental pollution and human health hazards. The proposed method exhibits high sensitivity, selectivity, and has been verified to be efficacious in real sample assays. This waste-to-resource approach presents a sustainable remedy for Sargassum handling and water quality surveillance, manifesting the potential of biowaste in environmental conservation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Hainan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 221QN0879) and Hainan Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 222RC656).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126283.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
